network.overall_strategy: This network targets threonine through a literature-grounded, bipartite prebiotic synthesis graph centered on the best-supported cyanosulfidic Strecker routes via lactaldehyde and glyceraldehyde, while also incorporating empirically observed but less selective source pathways from spark discharge, HCN polymer hydrolysis, and Bucherer-Bergs chemistry. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon feedstocks and concentration mechanisms; surface UV-exposed pools perform Cu-cyanide/H2S photoredox homologation, Strecker, and hydantoin chemistry; biochemical/prebiotic settings capture fragile threonine into peptides. To address known instability and stereochemical issues, the network treats most abiotic threonine-forming routes as racemic/threo-allothreo mixture-generating and includes rapid downstream incorporation into a dipeptide as a preservation/use pathway.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent micropores; H2-rich serpentinization-fed system; concentration by thermophoresis possible

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by hydrothermal reducing equivalents yields formate as an early C1 fixed-carbon product.
reactions.reasoning: Formate is a plausible hydrothermal feedstock in alkaline vent settings with FeS/Fe3S4 chimney minerals; greigite and mackinawite formation under Hadean vent conditions is documented, and vent micropores can strongly concentrate organics (White et al., 2015; Lane & Martin, 2012). This reaction is included as a literature-consistent hydrothermal carbon-fixation feeder rather than a direct threonine-forming step.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde from HCN

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox system in phosphate-buffered aqueous solution
conditions.other: UV irradiation; H2S as reductant; cyanosulfidic protometabolism

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven photoredox cycling of Cu(I) cyanide complexes reduces and homologates HCN-derived carbon, producing aldehydes including formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This is the core entry to the Sutherland cyanosulfidic network linking amino acid and nucleotide precursors. Ritson and Sutherland demonstrated UV/H2S/CuCN photoredox production of formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde from HCN-derived chemistry, and Patel et al. placed this in a broader integrated protometabolic framework (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Borate-stabilized sugar homologation to glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: borate-stabilized aldol chemistry under UV-exposed surface conditions
conditions.other: shallow pool chemistry; links sugar branch to amino acid branch

reactions.mechanism: Aldol-type homologation of lower aldehydes gives glyceraldehyde, with borate minerals stabilizing sugar products against uncontrolled formose scrambling.
reactions.reasoning: Glyceraldehyde is a key branch point in Sutherland-style nucleotide chemistry and a direct precursor to threonine aminonitrile. Borate minerals are included because they stabilize sugars in prebiotic surface settings and are explicitly required mineral agents for surface chemistry in this network. The central role of glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde as branch points is emphasized in the threonine literature review (Patel et al., 2015; Islam et al., 2017; de Marcellus et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin formation

conditions.other: cyanosulfidic pool; prelude to UV photoreduction

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic cyanide addition to acetaldehyde forms the cyanohydrin intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin is the experimentally demonstrated precursor to lactaldehyde in the Ritson-Sutherland route. This step is part of the same HCN/CuCN/H2S photoredox network and is directly relevant to threonine-specific carbon skeleton construction (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: UV photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation for 2 h; reported 19% yield of lactaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction of the nitrile-bearing cyanohydrin under H2S-driven UV conditions gives lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland experimentally showed photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde at 19% yield after 2 h irradiation. Lactaldehyde is the key threonine-specific aldehyde precursor in the best-characterized abiotic route (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.other: classic Strecker sequence via imine formation; racemic threonine/allothreonine precursor mixture expected

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine, then cyanide addition gives 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the canonical threonine aminonitrile-forming step from the literature. The deep research report identifies lactaldehyde Strecker chemistry as the most completely characterized route to threonine precursors and notes expected racemic/diastereomeric products (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Direct Strecker entry to threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: borate-stabilized sugar pool with phosphate buffering
conditions.other: surface cyanosulfidic setting; parallel entry point to same aminonitrile hub

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes Strecker-type amination/cyanation to yield the same C4 amino nitrile network node feeding threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. explicitly identify a parallel route in which glyceraldehyde reacts with NH3 and HCN to give the threonine aminonitrile, linking RNA precursor chemistry directly to amino acid precursor chemistry (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis; racemic threonine/allothreonine mixture expected overall from abiotic precursor pools

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydration and hydrolysis converts the aminonitrile into the amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker product 2-amino-3-hydroxybutyronitrile hydrolyzes to threonine/allothreonine. This transformation is part of the classical Strecker sequence described in the deep research report for threonine formation from lactaldehyde and glyceraldehyde routes (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin formation from lactaldehyde

conditions.other: ammonium carbonate equivalent from NH3 + CO2; hydantoin acts as thermodynamic sink

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde, cyanide, and ammonium carbonate equivalents form a hydantoin intermediate that stores the amino acid carbon skeleton in a more stable cyclic form.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research report identifies the Bucherer-Bergs route as a plausible threonine pathway in which lactaldehyde gives 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin before hydrolysis. Pulletikurti et al. demonstrated the general prebiotic relevance of hydantoin intermediates for amino-acid-forming chemistry, although threonine-specific implementation remains an inferred extension from the same framework; therefore this step is literature-guided but partly extrapolative (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner & Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin to threonine

conditions.other: hydantoin-opening hydrolysis in aqueous pool chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin releases the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis to amino acids is the defining final step of the Bucherer-Bergs pathway. The threonine-specific hydantoin is inferred in the deep research report from the demonstrated prebiotic Bucherer-Bergs framework and meteoritic hydantoin occurrence, so this is a necessary but partially extrapolated connector (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner & Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Spark discharge generation of HCN, aldehydes, and amino acid precursors from reduced gases

conditions.temperature: variable discharge plasma with aqueous trap
conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge over reduced gas mixtures; mineral surfaces as collection/concentration substrates
conditions.other: models CH4/N2/H2O/NH3 traces and CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S discharge experiments; product mixtures are racemic and complex

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge generates reactive nitriles, aldehydes, and radicals that collapse in the aqueous phase into Strecker precursors and amino acids, including threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Ring et al. first confirmed threonine/allothreonine in spark discharge products, Parker et al. detected threonine in Miller's H2S-containing apparatus, and Johnson et al. showed volcanic-discharge setups favor hydroxylated amino acids. This reaction is intentionally framed as a mixture-generating source rather than a mechanistically resolved dedicated threonine route, addressing prior overstatement issues (Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis to heterogeneous amino acid mixtures including threonine

conditions.catalyst: none intrinsic; minerals provide adsorption and photochemical context
conditions.other: long-duration polymerization/hydrolysis; low-selectivity heterogeneous residue chemistry

reactions.mechanism: HCN polymerizes into complex nitrile oligomers that on hydrolysis release a broad amino-acid and heterocycle mixture containing threonine among many products.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. reviewed HCN polymer experiments and found threonine among amino acids recovered from hydrolyzed HCN polymer products. This is explicitly a nonselective background source route, not a targeted threonine synthesis, included conservatively because the literature supports threonine occurrence but not clean mechanism or selectivity (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013; Miyakawa et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Conceptual aldol condensation of glycine and acetaldehyde to threonine

conditions.other: requires high glycine concentration or catalytic assistance; thermodynamically unfavorable; produces threonine/allothreonine mixture

reactions.mechanism: Retro-threonine-aldol logic is reversed: carbon-carbon bond formation between glycine-derived and acetaldehyde-derived fragments gives the threonine skeleton.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research report identifies the reverse of threonine aldol cleavage as a conceptually simple but thermodynamically unfavorable route with no purely prebiotic demonstration. It is therefore included as an explicitly speculative connector grounded in structural logic rather than direct experimental validation (Fesko, 2016; Koga & Naraoka, 2022).

reactions.name: Wet-dry clay-mediated peptide capture of threonine with glycine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on clay/mineral interfaces; capture of labile threonine into peptide pool

reactions.mechanism: Dehydration-condensation during wet-dry cycling forms a peptide bond, sequestering threonine into a more functionally relevant product pool.
reactions.reasoning: Given threonine's documented instability, rapid downstream utilization is chemically important. Wet-dry cycling and mineral surfaces promote peptide formation in prebiotic settings, and clay-assisted peptide synthesis is well documented. This reaction addresses the instability bottleneck highlighted in the report by converting threonine into a peptide product (Lahav et al., 1978; Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1998; Kitadai et al., 2015; Campbell et al., 2019; Truong et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate analog pool to alanine source

conditions.other: included as a conservative hydrothermal amino-acid source branch, not a threonine route

reactions.mechanism: Reduced iron/sulfide chemistry supports conversion of carbonyl/keto-acid-like feedstocks into amino acids such as alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids under Fe2+ and related hydrothermal conditions is established in principle, but the deep report cautions against overextending this directly to threonine. This branch is therefore used only to show hydrothermal amino-acid background chemistry and network connectivity, not as a dedicated threonine synthesis path (Muchowska et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024; Huber & Wächtershäuser, 2006).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN photoredox chemistry produces acetaldehyde; cyanohydrin photoreduction gives lactaldehyde; Strecker chemistry gives threonine aminonitrile, then hydrolysis gives threonine.

pathways.name: Glyceraldehyde Strecker route from cyanosulfidic sugar chemistry
pathways.description: HCN photoredox chemistry yields formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde; borate-stabilized homologation gives glyceraldehyde, which directly enters Strecker chemistry to the threonine aminonitrile and then threonine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs route from lactaldehyde
pathways.description: The canonical cyanosulfidic route is diverted from lactaldehyde into a hydantoin thermodynamic sink, which hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge direct threonine source
pathways.description: Electrical discharge of reduced gases generates a complex racemic amino acid mixture including threonine detected experimentally.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge to HCN-acetaldehyde then lactaldehyde Strecker
pathways.description: Spark discharge supplies HCN and acetaldehyde that can feed the better-resolved lactaldehyde Strecker sequence in surface pools.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge to glyceraldehyde branch via cyanosulfidic surface chemistry
pathways.description: Spark-generated HCN feeds surface photoredox chemistry, producing glycolaldehyde/formaldehyde and then glyceraldehyde, which enters the direct Strecker route.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysate threonine source
pathways.description: HCN polymerization and hydrolysis yield a heterogeneous amino-acid mixture containing threonine as a low-selectivity source.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer to cyanosulfidic aldehyde pool then glyceraldehyde Strecker
pathways.description: HCN-rich surface reservoirs participate both in polymerization/hydrolysis chemistry and in cyanosulfidic aldehyde generation, allowing a second HCN-derived route to the threonine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Aldol-like glycine plus acetaldehyde route
pathways.description: A speculative but literature-discussed reverse-threonine-aldol logic combines glycine and acetaldehyde to give threonine under concentrated prebiotic conditions.

pathways.name: Rapid peptide capture of newly formed threonine
pathways.description: Any surface-formed threonine is rapidly incorporated into a dipeptide during wet-dry cycling, addressing its poor long-term stability.
